with AI
Designing with AI as a system
Core: Make · Prompting · Workshop   ·   Bonus: Claude Code + MCP
Day 2 recap
yesterday
→ Built a clear mental model: plugins → connectors → agents → skills (skills = reusable workflows + better context control)
→ Practiced a brainstorming skill to structure decisions before execution
→ Built an icon-generator skill using reference PNGs + Gemini (via OpenRouter)
→ Learned the iterate + evaluate loop (LLM + human criteria), plus key hygiene: watch context/costs, don't ship API keys
"Every pause is scripted.
Every stammer is a stage direction."
— Winston Churchill (paraphrase)
Engineered spontaneity = prompts as reusable systems.
What you'll leave with
A multi-section prototype, shareable in <30 min
From idea to shareable link — inside Figma.
Make outputs token-aware — and know the limits
Design-system-aware output, when you prompt right.
Know when Make stops paying off
A decision skill — not a failure point.
Today's agenda
1 Figma Make 20 min
2 Prompting for Make 15 min
3 Workshop 30 min
4 Bonus: Claude Code + MCP 10 min

Section 01
Figma Make
What it is · What it does · Where it breaks
What is Figma Make?
🔵 What it is
An AI tool inside Figma that turns designs into working, interactive code — automatically.
💡 Analogy
You sketch the floor plan. Make assembles the furniture — in real time, while you watch.
Quick prototype, not production
✓  Great for
Quick ideation
Few-page flows
Concept validation
Stakeholder preview / UXR
✗  Not great for
50-screen refactors
Production handoff
Complex multi-page apps
Under the hood: an LLM with Figma-aware context — strong with text/layout, weaker with components.
What Make is actually good at
Quick ideation & prototyping
From prompt to interactive prototype in minutes.
Import back to Figma design
Bring your Make output into Figma with the handy import feature.
Templates for teams
Reusable prototype templates — not a component-library replacement.
Best results when you attach a kit and name tokens explicitly.
The risks that change your behavior
Looks right, isn't always right
Polished UI can hide UX or requirements errors.
Invents UI you didn't ask for
Extra nav, phantom CTAs, invented sections.
Small tweaks → big breaks
Destructive iteration risk — one prompt can undo a lot.
Partial design-system fidelity
Looks on-brand but misses component-level precision.
Other things to know
Low debuggability
When it's wrong, it's hard to diagnose why. Often trial and error.
Heavy prompting can erase the speed advantage
The more structure you need, the less "instant" it feels.
Not production handoff-ready
Good for prototyping — less reliable as source of truth.
None of these are dealbreakers — they're reasons to prompt deliberately.
Deep Dive
Figma Make UI walkthrough
An in-depth look at Figma Make and its features.
Section 02
Write better prompts
Prompts are systems, not requests.
What not to write
"Build me a landing page for a new vertical called 'road trip' where users can book a carshare to an Airbnb destination. Make sure it is done in the Airbnb style."
Vague scope — which sections? how many?
"Airbnb style" — vibe ≠ spec
No design system constraints
No "do not" guardrails
What makes a prompt work
Shared context block
One framing statement prepended to every prompt. Keeps scope, tokens, and rules consistent.
Name every element
No vibes, no metaphors. Heading, subheading, primary CTA — not "make it feel welcoming."
One prompt per section
Each prompt covers one visual region. Errors don't compound.
Use Airbnb kit. Semantic tokens only. No shadows.
One section only. Heading, subheading, primary CTA.
Prompt Guidelines
01
Shared context block
One framing statement prepended to every prompt — keeps spacing, tokens, and scope consistent without repeating.
Context: Airbnb listing page, 375px, Airbnb DS tokens. No hardcoded values.
02
One prompt per section
Each prompt covers exactly one visual region — errors don't compound and constraints get respected.
Build the hero section only. Full-bleed image, search bar at 60% height.
03
Elements listed, not described
Name every UI item explicitly — headline, subheadline, CTA — not as a feeling or outcome.
Include: headline, subheadline, primary CTA, trust badge row (3 icons + labels).
04
Spatial layout instructions
"Centered", "pinned to the bottom", "side by side", "top half" — remove all structural ambiguity.
Search bar: centered, pinned 60% from top. Full-width pill shape on mobile.
05
Named tokens throughout
Every colour and type decision references a semantic token (-color-bg-brand, heading-large) not a visual description.
bg: -color-bg-primary  button: -color-action-primary  label: body-small
06
"Do not" rules in every section
Each prompt closes with constraints — no shadows, no invented elements, no hardcoded values.
Do not add shadows, gradients, or any element not listed above.
07
Progressive and reviewable
The sequence is ordered and numbered — check each section before building the next.
Step 1 of 4: hero only. Confirm before moving to the search results grid.
08
Provide visuals
A Figma design or inspiration screenshot helps the LLM build exactly what you want.
[screenshot attached] Match this layout — DS tokens throughout, no overrides.
What good iteration looks like
Prompt
One section at a time
→
Review
Does it match the brief?
→
Refine
clarify in chat
point-edit
HTML tweak (saves tokens)
→
Re-test
Mobile + desktop view
Stay in this loop. Don't restart from scratch.
Live: I'll demo the loop in 30 sec
Prompt Iteration Examples
Difference in context
and prompting
Bad prompt
Bad prompt with DS attached
Good prompt with DS attached
Final product after tweaking the design
about 10 prompts
Section 03
Your turn
30 minutes · Prompt → Prototype → Share
Workshop checklist
1Rewrite the prompt
Download the "figma make prompt guide" skill from the Skool link
Double tap to open the skill — it will open in Claude Code → add to library
Type /figma-make-prompt-guide press "tab" then your prompt
Type your idea — skill guides you through improving it
2Create a Make file + orient the UI
Open the Airbnb Figma file and publish it as a library
Open Figma Make
Press the lego brick icon
Bottom right → Create kit
Attach your Airbnb library
You're ready to prompt with a preset design system
3Prompt-to-prototype
Write your prompt with the Claude skill
Copy it into Figma Make
Use a clarification prompt in Make chat to improve the design
Use point-and-edit
Edit text directly in the HTML (saves tokens)
Switch between mobile and desktop view
Publish your first Figma Make kit
Publish your prototype as a template
Import the design back into Figma
Copy link to the figma make prototype
Definition of done
1 prototype + 1 iteration + 1 import + 1 share link
Still on step 2 at 15 min? Jump to step 3b.
No kit? Use default — practice prompt rules with text labels.
Bonus (if time)
Claude Code
+ Figma MCP
Same goal, different workflow
If we skip this — you have everything you need for Make.
Continue here if you want production-grade workflows.
MCP — a protocol that lets Claude read live context from external tools — here, your Figma file.
CLI — you type prompts in the terminal instead of a chat UI — more control, less magic.
Setup checklist: Claude Code + Figma MCP
Prerequisites
✅ Claude Pro, Max, or Team account (or API access)
✅ Figma Full or Dev seat — Starter = only 6 MCP calls/month
✅ Terminal open — ⌘ Space → "Terminal"
1Install Claude Code (5 min)
Native installer — no Node.js, no npm needed
Run: curl -fsSL https://claude.ai/install.sh | sh
Verify: claude --version — should see a version number
If not: open a new terminal window and retry
2Authenticate (3 min)
Run: claude
Browser opens → log in with Anthropic account → Allow
Terminal returns once authenticated
3Install the Figma plugin (5 min)
Run: claude plugin install figma@claude-plugins-official
Press Enter to confirm
Restart: Option C × 2, then type claude again
4Authenticate with Figma (5 min)
Type /mcp → select Figma → Authenticate
Browser opens → Allow Access
Terminal confirms: Authentication successful. Connected to figma
5Verify connection (2 min)
Type /mcp → Figma shows as connected under Installed tab
Type /plugin → confirm Figma plugin is installed
6Test it (5 min)
Open any Figma file in your browser
Select a frame, copy the URL from the address bar
In Claude Code, type:
Get the design context for this frame: [paste URL]
MCP is link-based — the URL scopes Claude to that exact node
Quick reference
Open Claude Code → claude
Check MCP → /mcp
View plugins → /plugin
Re-auth Figma → /mcp → Figma → Authenticate
Designing with Claude
Create a folder on your machine named airbnb-lp
Add the Airbnb design system file in that folder
Open this folder in Claude Code
Go to Figma — the page where you imported your Airbnb landing page
Select any frame and press ⌘ L to copy the link to that frame
Go back to Claude and paste the link in the chat
Prompt Claude: Build this Figma design using the design system in this folder and open it in my browser when done [figma link]
Make changes using the same prompt techniques as in Figma Make
Bonus: Prompt Claude to make changes to the design directly in Figma
The point
Switch between tools depending on the level of fidelity you want for your prototype.
Extracting your Design System
Via Figma MCP
Using the Figma MCP, read this file: [figma link]

Extract the following and output it as structured Markdown:

— Component names and descriptions
— Key variants and states per component
— Any documented usage rules or notes
— Token names if visible in variable definitions
3rd Party Option
Firecrawl
Scrapes websites and extracts their design system. Useful when a component library isn't in Figma.
firecrawl.dev
More control or Quick exploration
Make gives you speed.
Claude gives you precision.
Same goal — different lever.
Use both depending on the job.
The fact most designers don't know
Paste a Figma frame URL. Claude reads the actual components, tokens, and layout — and builds with that context.
If we're out of time — this is the one slide to take home.
Quick reference
Figma Make Claude Code + MCP
Speed Fast Slower setup
Design system Partial — best with tokens + kits More precise — scoped to frame/component URLs
Multi-screen Limited Scalable
Best for Ideation + stakeholder preview Complex prototypes + repeatable workflows
Make for speed. Claude for control.
Q&A — 10 minutes
You built a prototype system today — not just a screen.
